---
title: "Descriptive Statistics"
subtitle: "Summarise your data with means, standard deviations, and grouped summaries."
toc: false
---

<details class="topic-card" open>
<summary>Basics (when to use cat(), signif(), round())</summary>
<div class="topic-card-body">

Use these three together when reporting results clearly:

- `cat()` for readable text output
- `signif()` for significant digits (good for scientific reporting)
- `round()` for fixed decimal places (good for tables)

```{webr-r}
x <- c(99.83, 100.12, 100.29, 99.67, 100.41)

cat("Raw mean:        ", mean(x), "\n")
cat("Using signif(4): ", signif(mean(x), 4), "\n")
cat("Using round(2):  ", round(mean(x), 2), "\n")
```

</div>
</details>

<details class="topic-card">
<summary>Frequency & Distribution</summary>
<div class="topic-card-body">

## count()
Use `count()` to build a quick frequency table.
This is useful when you want exact counts for each category.

```{webr-r}
library(tidyverse)

df_cat <- tibble::tibble(batch = c("A", "A", "A", "B", "B", "C"))

# Frequency table for categories
df_cat %>%
  count(batch)
```

## Barchart
A bar chart visualizes categorical frequencies.
Use it to compare how many observations belong to each group.

```{webr-r}
library(tidyverse)

df_cat <- tibble::tibble(batch = c("A", "A", "A", "B", "B", "C"))

# Bar chart (categorical frequencies)
barplot(table(df_cat$batch),
        main = "Batch frequencies",
        xlab = "Batch",
        ylab = "Count")
```

## Histogram
A histogram visualizes the distribution of a numeric variable.
Use it to see where values cluster and how spread out they are.

```{webr-r}
library(tidyverse)

df_cat <- tibble::tibble(batch = c("A", "A", "A", "B", "B", "C"))

# Histogram (numeric frequencies)
x <- c(9.8, 10.2, 10.5, 10.1, 10.4, 10.0, 9.9, 10.3, 10.6, 10.2)
hist(x,
     main = "Histogram of values",
     xlab = "Value",
     col = "lightgray",
     border = "white")
```

</div>
</details>

<details class="topic-card">
<summary>Central Tendency</summary>
<div class="topic-card-body">

Central tendency describes the "typical" value in a dataset.
The two most common measures are mean and median.

- Mean: arithmetic average of all values
- Median: middle value after sorting the data

If the distribution is fairly symmetric, mean and median are often similar.
  If there are outliers or strong skew, the median usually reflects the center better.

## Mean

Use the mean when you want to include all observations in the summary.
Because every value contributes, extreme values can pull it up or down.

```{webr-r}
x <- c(10, 11, 10, 12, 60)
cat("Mean:  ", mean(x), "\n")
```

## Median

Use the median when you want a robust center that is less affected by outliers.
It is often preferred for skewed data.

```{webr-r}
x <- c(10, 11, 10, 12, 60)
cat("Median:", median(x), "\n")
```

</div>
</details>

<details class="topic-card">
<summary>Variability</summary>
<div class="topic-card-body">

Variability describes how spread out values are around the center.
Use these measures together to understand both total spread and typical spread.

## Extrema
Use `min()` and `max()` to report range endpoints.
They are useful for quickly spotting the lowest and highest observed values.

```{webr-r}
x <- c(7.2, 6.8, 7.5, 6.9, 7.1)
cat("Min:", min(x), "\n")
cat("Max:", max(x), "\n")
```

## Standard deviation
Standard deviation (`sd`) is the typical distance of values from the mean.
A larger SD means values are more dispersed.

```{webr-r}
x <- c(101.2, 100.8, 101.5, 100.9, 101.1)
cat("SD:      ", signif(sd(x), 4), "\n")
```

## Variance
Variance (`var`) is the squared version of spread around the mean.
It is less intuitive than SD but important in many statistical methods.

```{webr-r}
x <- c(101.2, 100.8, 101.5, 100.9, 101.1)
cat("Variance:", signif(var(x), 4), "\n")
```

## Quantiles & Interquartile range (IQR)
IQR shows the spread of the middle 50% of the data.
Quantiles provide cut points (for example 25%, 50%, and 75%).
These are robust to outliers compared with range-based measures.

```{webr-r}
x <- c(12, 14, 15, 16, 18, 19, 21)
cat("IQR: ", IQR(x), "\n")
quantile(x, probs = c(0.25, 0.5, 0.75))
```

</div>
</details>

<details class="topic-card">
<summary>summarize()</summary>
<div class="topic-card-body">

Use `summarize()` for whole-table summaries, and combine with `group_by()` for per-group summaries.

```{webr-r}
library(dplyr)

df <- tibble::tibble(
  batch = c("A", "A", "B", "B", "B"),
  value = c(9.8, 10.2, 10.5, 10.1, 10.4)
)

# Overall summary
df %>%
  summarize(n = n(), mean_value = mean(value), sd_value = sd(value))

# By-group summary
df %>%
  group_by(batch) %>%
  summarize(n = n(), mean_value = mean(value), sd_value = sd(value))
```

</div>
</details>
